locations.raw_source_name: Journal of magnetic resonance imaging : JMRI

crossref.abstract: <jats:sec><jats:title>Background</jats:title><jats:p>Multiparametric MRI may cause overdiagnosis of clinically significant prostate cancer (csPCa) with the Prostate Imaging Reporting and Data System version 2.1 (PI‐RADS v2.1).</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Objectives</jats:title><jats:p>To investigate the diagnostic performance of stiffness as a standalone and complementary marker to PI‐RADS v2.1 for diagnosing csPCa.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Study Type</jats:title><jats:p>Prospective.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Subjects</jats:title><jats:p>One hundred forty‐seven participants with pathologically confirmed prostate lesions (≥1 cm), including 71 with csPCa.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Field Strength/Sequence</jats:title><jats:p>T1‐weighted fast spin‐echo, T2‐weighted fast spin‐echo, single‐shot echo‐planar diffusion‐weighted imaging, fast 3D gradient‐echo T1‐weighted dynamic contrast‐enhanced imaging, and 3D single‐shot spin‐echo based echo‐planar MR elastography at 3.0 T.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Assessment</jats:title><jats:p>The PI‐RADS v2.1 score was assessed by three radiologists independently. Lesion shear stiffness (SS) values at 60 Hz and 90 Hz were measured. A modified PI‐RADS integrating stiffness with PI‐RADS v2.1 was developed. Diagnostic performance for csPCa was compared between stiffness, PI‐RADS v2.1 and the modified PI‐RADS.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Statistical Test</jats:title><jats:p>Spearman's correlation, Fleiss κ and intraclass correlation, Pearson correlation, one‐way analysis of variance, area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), and the Delong test. Significance level was <jats:italic>P</jats:italic> < 0.05.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Results</jats:title><jats:p>In the peripheral zone, csPCa (N = 35) had significantly higher SS than non‐csPCa at 60 Hz (3.22 ± 0.66 kPa vs. 2.56 ± 0.56 kPa) and at 90 Hz (5.64 ± 1.30 kPa vs. 4.48 ± 0.84 kPa). PI‐RADS v2.1 showed 100% sensitivity, 58% specificity, and 0.79 AUC for detecting csPCa. SS achieved 97% sensitivity, 52% specificity, and 0.80 AUC at 60 Hz, while SS had 63% sensitivity, 87% specificity, and 0.78 AUC at 90 Hz. The modified PI‐RADS, combing SS at 60 Hz with PI‐RADS v2.1, resulted in a significantly increased AUC (0.86) compared to that of PI‐RADS v2.1, with a sensitivity of 97% and specificity of 75%.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Data Conclusion</jats:title><jats:p>Stiffness can help identifying csPCa in the peripheral zone. Combining stiffness with the PI‐RADS v2.1 improved the diagnostic accuracy and specificity for csPCa.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Evidence Level</jats:title><jats:p>1</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Technical Efficacy</jats:title><jats:p>Stage 2</jats:p></jats:sec>
title: Magnetic Resonance Elastography Combined With <scp>PI</scp>‐<scp>RADS</scp> v2.1 for the Identification of Clinically Significant Prostate Cancer
